1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 12	## Structural problem
13	A (pre)sheaf whose value at `X` is defined via the **over-category / slice** `Over X`
14	(`ℋom(A,B)(V) = (A|_{Over V} ⟶ B|_{Over V})`, a compatible family over all `W ≤ V`)
15	must be shown to **commute with restriction along an open immersion** `U ↪ X`.
16	Concretely close `(pushforward β).obj (dual A) ≅ dual ((pushforward β).obj A)`, where
17	`dual = internalHom(−, 𝟙_)` is the slice internal hom in `PresheafOfModules` over the
18	ringed space `(X, 𝒪_X)`. Mathlib has no MonoidalCategory / monoidal-closed structure on
19	`PresheafOfModules`, so the module-valued internal hom is genuinely new — but the
20	over-category SHAPE and the open-immersion reindexing are not.
21	
22	## Analogues (summary)
23	
24	| Analogue | Domain | Porting cost | Verdict |
25	|---|---|---|---|
26	| `TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence` (`Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.Over`) | topology / sheaves on spaces | low | ANALOGUE_FOUND |
27	| `CategoryTheory.Sites.Over` (`overEquiv`, `J.over`, `Sheaf.over`, `iteratedSliceEquiv` dense subsite, `overMapPullback{Id,Comp,assoc}`) | category theory / sites | medium | ANALOGUE_FOUND |
28	| `CategoryTheory.Sites.SheafHom` (`presheafHom`/`sheafHom`, `presheafHom_isSheafFor`) | category theory / internal hom of sheaves | low–medium | ANALOGUE_FOUND |
29	| `CategoryTheory.LocallyCartesianClosed.ExponentiableMorphism` | category theory / LCC, dependent product | high | PARTIAL_ANALOGUE |
30	
31	## Top suggestion
32	**The base `Opens X` is a poset = thin category — every hom-set is a subsingleton.** The
33	prover's "~150–300 LOC `Over.map` pseudofunctor coherence" estimate, and every
34	`hom_app_heq`+`subst` fight recorded in iters 218–220, is pain inherited from mirroring
35	Mathlib's GENERAL-site `Sites/SheafHom.lean` + `Sites/Over.lean`, where hom-sets are
36	arbitrary. On `Opens X` the coherence squares (`Over.mapId`/`Over.mapComp`,
37	`overMapPullback_assoc`) commute automatically by `Subsingleton.elim`/`Subsingleton.helim`
38	(`Over.OverMorphism.ext` reduces an `Over`-hom equality to its underlying poset hom,
39	itself a subsingleton).
40	
41	Concretely, in order:
42	1. Port `TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence U : Over U ≌ Opens ↥U` — already in
43	   Mathlib at `Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean` (~25 LOC). This IS the
44	   open-immersion slice-site reindexing equivalence the prover wanted.

48	   Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology U) A`"). Fill it using the ready-made
49	   continuity/cocontinuity + dense-subsite instances in `Sites/Over.lean` and
50	   `Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv` (`Sites/DenseSubsite/Basic.lean`).
51	3. Reduce the residual `(pushforward β)(dual A) ≅ dual(pushforward β A)` to the poset
52	   slice identity "`Over V` in `Opens U` = `Over V` in `Opens X` for `V ≤ U`" (the
53	   down-set `↓V` is literally the same); the comparison functor is
54	   `overEquivalence`/`iteratedSliceEquiv`, naturality is `Subsingleton.elim`.

56	First Mathlib file to read: `Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean`. First project file to
57	touch: the file defining `Scheme.Modules.dual` / `internalHom`. Expected cost is WELL
58	under the 150–300 LOC general-`Over.map` estimate because thinness kills the coherence

92	### Q2 — `presheafHom` + `existsUnique_gluing` is correct; finish with `sheafHomSectionsEquiv`

94	`presheafHom (M.val.presheaf) (N.val.presheaf)` is a `Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Type`, a sheaf via
95	`Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom` (needs `N.val` a sheaf — it is). Packaged as `TopCat.Sheaf (Type) X`,
96	`existsUnique_gluing` (`Topology/Sheaves/SheafCondition/UniqueGluing.lean:180`) applies: its
97	instance side-conditions (`HasLimitsOfSize Type`, `forget` reflects/preserves limits) are
98	trivially met for `Type`. So the **cover→⊤** gluing step is `existsUnique_gluing` over the
99	cover `U` with `⨆ U = ⊤`.

101	The **⊤→morphism** step is *not* a hand-unfold of the `⊤`-section: use
102	`presheafHomSectionsEquiv` (L80) / `sheafHomSectionsEquiv` (L241). For `Opens X`, `⊤` is

105	hand. Recommended idiom: `existsUnique_gluing` (cover→⊤) ∘ `presheafHomSectionsEquiv` (⊤→hom)
106	∘ `Scheme.Modules.homMk` (linearity). All three are Mathlib/already-built; no parallel API.

113	local datum `fᵢ : M.restrict (Uᵢ).ι ⟶ N.restrict (Uᵢ).ι` lives over the **open-immersion
114	pullback** (`Opens ↥Uᵢ`). Converting `fᵢ` into a section of `presheafHom` over `Uᵢ` — the
115	`localSection` construction, with the `eqToHom`-conjugation along `(Uᵢ).ι ''ᵁ ((Uᵢ).ι ⁻¹ᵁ V) = V`
116	— is the **section-direction realization of the un-done `Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean` TODO**.
117	It is the same gap-class as the C-bridge's `(pushforward β)(dual A) ≅ dual(pushforward β A)`

2941	  open cover of \(X\). Suppose given, for each \(i\), a morphism
2942	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that
2943	  for all \(i, j\) the restrictions of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree.

2948	\begin{proof}
2949	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
2950	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
2951	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
2952	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.
2953	
2954	  \emph{(i) Glue the underlying ab-sheaf morphism.} Forget \(M\) and \(N\) to their
2955	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
2956	  \(\mathcal{H} := \mathtt{presheafHom}\,(M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\,
2957	  (N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
2958	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
2959	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
2960	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
2961	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
2962	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
2963	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
2964	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
2965	  strictly more bookkeeping.
2966	
2967	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}
2968	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,U_i)\)
2969	  manufactured from \(f_i\). Its component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given
2970	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
2971	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
2972	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
2973	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole
2974	  construction is concentrated in the \emph{naturality field} of
2975	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
2976	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
2977	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece that should be
2978	  built and verified first, as a standalone axiom-clean lemma.
2979	
2980	  The remaining four sub-steps are mechanical: (a) the
2981	  cocycle/\(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition on the family
2982	  \((\mathtt{localSection}\,i)_i\), which is exactly the assumed agreement of \(f_i\)
2983	  and \(f_j\) on the overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\); (b) gluing via
2984	  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
2985	  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}
2986	  \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of the underlying presheaves; (c) the
2987	  sectionwise linearity check; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
2988	  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).
2989	
2990	  \emph{(ii) Promote to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear.} The linearity equation
2991	  \(g(r \cdot m) = r \cdot g(m)\) is a sectionwise identity between two sections of a
2992	  separated presheaf. It holds on each \(U_i\), because there \(g\) restricts to the
2993	  module morphism \(f_i\); since the two sides agree on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) and the
2994	  ambient presheaf is separated, they agree globally. Packaging \(g\) together with this
2995	  global linearity through the scheme-level wrapper \cref{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
2996	  (\(\mathtt{homMk}\)) yields a morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
2997	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); its restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\) and its
2998	  uniqueness are both inherited from the ab-sheaf amalgamation of step (i).
2999	
3000	  This gluing engine is substantially larger than a routine glue: realistically
3001	  \(\sim\!120\text{--}190\) lines, dominated by the \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality
3002	  field and the convert-and-recover bookkeeping. It is therefore to be scoped as a
3003	  multi-piece build --- with \(\mathtt{localSection}\), together with its naturality,
3004	  built as a standalone axiom-clean lemma first --- rather than a one-shot. The step
3005	  computes no tensor stalk --- it is purely a statement about gluing morphisms of
3006	  sheaves --- and so it does not re-enter the abandoned tensor-stalk commutation.
3007	\end{proof}

\begin{definition}
[Precomposition equivalence on dual sections]
  \label{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}
  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dualPrecompEquiv}
  \uses{def:presheaf_dual}
  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings and let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\)
  be an isomorphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. On a fixed open \(U\), precomposition
  with \(e\) gives an \(R(U)\)-linear equivalence between the dual sections
  \[
    \mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U \;\colon\;
      \bigl(M'|_U \to R|_U\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \bigl(M|_U \to R|_U\bigr),
      \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; \varphi \circ e|_U,
  \]
  with inverse \(\psi \mapsto \psi \circ (e^{-1})|_U\). This is the section-level core of
  the contravariant functoriality of the presheaf dual \(M \mapsto M^\vee\)
  (\cref{def:presheaf_dual}) in isomorphisms: precomposing a functional on \(M'\) with
  the comparison isomorphism produces a functional on \(M\), \(R(U)\)-linearly and
  invertibly.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[The presheaf dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(M'^\vee \cong M^\vee\)]
  \label{def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso}
  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dualIsoOfIso}
  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}
  An isomorphism \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules induces a
  canonical isomorphism of presheaf duals
  \[
    \mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e \;\colon\; M'^\vee \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M^\vee
  \]
  --- note the reversal of direction, the dual being contravariant --- assembled
  open-by-open from the precomposition equivalences
  \(\mathtt{dualPrecompEquiv}\,e\,U\) of \cref{def:presheaf_dual_precomp_equiv}.
  Naturality in \(U\), i.e.\ compatibility of these sectionwise equivalences with
  restriction, is the routine check that precomposition by \(e\) commutes with
  restricting functionals.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}
[The sheaf-level dual carries an iso \(M \cong M'\) to an iso \(\mathtt{dual}\,M' \cong \mathtt{dual}\,M\)]
  \label{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dualIsoOfIso}
  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) an isomorphism in
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of sheaf-level
  duals
  \[
    \mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e \;\colon\; \mathtt{dual}\,M' \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
      \mathtt{dual}\,M,
  \]
  obtained by sheafifying the presheaf isomorphism \(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\,e\) of
  \cref{def:presheaf_dual_iso_of_iso} through the sheaf-level dual of
  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}. It is the dual (contravariant) analogue of the
  functoriality of the tensor product in isomorphisms: where an isomorphism of a tensor
  factor induces an isomorphism of tensor products, an isomorphism \(M \cong M'\)
  induces an isomorphism of duals in the reverse direction.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}
[Open-immersion slice-site sheaf equivalence (the shared slice bridge)]
  \label{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overSliceSheafEquiv}
  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is NOT an external literature
  % citation, so it carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). This lemma
  % completes the documented TODO at Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean, lines
  % 19--22, reproduced here for orientation:
  %   "show that both functors of the equivalence overEquivalence U are continuous
  %    and induce an equivalence between Sheaf ((Opens.grothendieckTopology X).over U) A
  %    and Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology U) A for any category A."
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
  immersion \(\iota : U \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing equivalence
  \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U : \mathtt{Over}\,U \simeq \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\)
  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) and the opens of the subspace
  \(\widetilde{U}\) --- upgrades to an \emph{equivalence of sheaf categories}
  \[
    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)\,A
      \;\;\simeq\;\;
    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,U\bigr)\,A
  \]
  for any target category \(A\), compatible with the module-pullback comparison
  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). This completes the Mathlib TODO recorded at
  \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22). It is the \emph{shared root}
  underlying both the restriction-compatibility isomorphism for the dual
  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) and the morphism-descent / gluing engine
  (\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}): both reduce to identifying a
  slice-indexed family of sections over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) with an
  open-immersion-indexed family over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\), and that
  identification is exactly this equivalence.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  The functor \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\) realises \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) as a
  \emph{dense subsite} of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\): every open of the subspace
  \(\widetilde{U}\) is covered by down-sets \(\{V : V \le U\}\), and these down-sets are
  precisely the objects of the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\). One therefore applies the
  dense-subsite transfer \(\mathtt{Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv}\): a continuous,
  cocontinuous, dense inclusion of sites induces an equivalence of the associated sheaf
  categories. The continuity and cover-lifting hypotheses are supplied by the slice-site
  instances of \(\mathtt{Sites/Over}\) (the Grothendieck topology
  \(J.\mathtt{over}\,U\) and its comparison with the topology pulled back along
  \(\iota\)).

  The over-category coherence that is laborious for a general site --- the pseudofunctor
  identities \(\mathtt{Over.mapId}\), \(\mathtt{Over.mapComp}\) and the associativity
  \(\mathtt{overMapPullback\_assoc}\) --- is here \emph{automatic}: because
  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, every hom-set is a subsingleton, so each such
  square commutes by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (and its heterogeneous form
  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.helim}\) where source and target objects differ only up to a
  propositional equality of opens). The sole sectionwise content is the down-set
  identification
  \[
    \iota_{!}\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) \;=\; V
    \qquad (V \le U),
  \]
  i.e.\ the image under the open immersion of the preimage of an open contained in \(U\)
  is that open again. Transporting sections along this equality and invoking the
  dense-subsite equivalence yields the stated sheaf equivalence, compatible with
  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) by the same thinness argument.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
        lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an open \(U\) with a
  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The crux is the
  restriction-compatibility isomorphism
  \[
    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
  \]
  for an open immersion \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\), built as a \emph{verbatim mirror}
  of the closed linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, with the tensor product
  \(\otimes\) replaced throughout by the internal hom / dual. Three steps are reused
  \emph{unchanged}, because both \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) and the tensor
  \(M \otimes_X N\) are of the form \(\mathtt{sheafification}.\mathrm{obj}\) of a
  presheaf:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item reduce restriction to the abstract pullback,
      \((-)|_f \cong (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via
      \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\);
    \item move the pullback inside the sheafification,
      \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \cong
      \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\), via
      \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\);
    \item strip the outer sheafification with \(\mathtt{.mapIso}\), reducing the claim
      to the presheaf-level comparison.
  \end{enumerate}
  The presheaf residual then closes exactly as for the tensor: the open-immersion
  pushforward comparison \emph{H1}, \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) (the identification of two left adjoints of the same
  pushforward, \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) together with
  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\)), is reused \emph{verbatim}; it is composed
  with the dual analogue \emph{H2$'$} of the tensor's ring-iso commutation, namely the
  ring-iso/dual commutation \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv} along the
  open-immersion structure-sheaf ring isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). Under the
  trivialisation this gives
  \[
    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U
      \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_U\bigr)
      \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U,
  \]
  the last identification being evaluation at \(1\), the canonical iso of the dual of a
  free rank-one module with itself. Hence \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of
  rank one.

  No tensor stalk, and no whiskering \(M \triangleleft \eta\) of the sheafification
  unit, is ever invoked: the comparison reduces to slice / \(\mathtt{Over}\)-reindexing
  under the open immersion (a structural reindexing, not a stalk) together with the

  covariant. (Equivalently, this is item~2 of Stacks
  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}, tag 01CR: the dual of an invertible

\end{proof}

  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an open \(U\) with a
  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The crux is the
  restriction-compatibility isomorphism
  \[
    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
  \]
  for an open immersion \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\). Its first three steps run exactly
  as for the closed linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, because both
  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) and the tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) are of the form
  \(\mathtt{sheafification}.\mathrm{obj}\) of a presheaf:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item reduce restriction to the abstract pullback,
      \((-)|_f \cong (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via
      \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\);
    \item move the pullback inside the sheafification,
      \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \cong
      \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\), via
      \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\);
    \item strip the outer sheafification with \(\mathtt{.mapIso}\), reducing the claim
      to the presheaf-level comparison.
  \end{enumerate}
  These three steps, together with the open-immersion pushforward comparison \emph{H1},
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) (the identification of
  two left adjoints of the same pushforward, \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\)
  together with \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\)), are reused \emph{verbatim} from
  the tensor and do typecheck for the dual.

  Here the parallel with the tensor \emph{ends}. After H1 the residual to close is
  \[
    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,A)
      \;\cong\;
    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,A\bigr),
  \]
  i.e.\ the open-immersion pushforward commutes with the presheaf dual. For the tensor
  the corresponding residual closed through the ring-iso commutation, because
  \(M \otimes_X N\) is \emph{sectionwise} \(M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)\) --- a literal
  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\)-image, to which the strong-monoidal comparison applies.
  The dual is \emph{not} of this form: \(\mathtt{dual} = \mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
  is the \emph{slice} internal hom, whose value at an open \(V\) is a module of
  morphisms over the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), not a sectionwise
  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\)-image. Consequently the ring-iso/dual commutation
  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv} is only the \emph{ModuleCat-level shadow}
  of the comparison; it does \emph{not} by itself discharge the residual, and the naive
  ``verbatim mirror'' \emph{H2$'$} of the tensor's ring-iso step does not close it.

  The residual is instead discharged by the shared slice-site sheaf equivalence
  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}. Under that equivalence the slice internal
  hom computed over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\) inside \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) corresponds to the
  slice internal hom computed over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\); because
  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, the comparison is precisely the poset-thin
  reindexing of \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\), all of whose naturality squares commute
  by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The ring-iso/dual commutation
  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv} survives only as the sectionwise algebraic
  shadow carried along inside this transport. Combining the equivalence with the
  trivialisation gives
  \[
    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U
      \;\cong\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_U\bigr)
      \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U,
  \]
  the last identification being evaluation at \(1\), the canonical iso of the dual of a
  free rank-one module with itself. Hence \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of
  rank one.

  No tensor stalk, and no whiskering \(M \triangleleft \eta\) of the sheafification
  unit, is ever invoked: the comparison reduces to the open-immersion
  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport of \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
  (a structural reindexing on a thin poset, not a stalk). (Equivalently, this is item~2
  of Stacks Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}, tag 01CR: the dual of an
  invertible module is invertible.)
\end{proof}
